Title: Carotenoid Metabolism: Biosynthesis, Regulation, and Beyond

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Cysteine-rich zinc finger domain-containing OR protein with CxxCxGxG domain specific to DnaJ-like molecular chaperones
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes high β-carotene and total carotenoid accumulation by inducing chromoplast biogenesis and increasing sink strength
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pro-vitamin A nutrition; antioxidant and health-promoting carotenoid accumulation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; transgenic expression; carotenoid measurement; plastid ultrastructure
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for carotenoid biofortification by enhancing sink strength and chromoplast formation; complementary to pathway engineering/flux enhancement
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Or mutation confers high β-carotene accumulation in cauliflower. 2. OR induces differentiation of non-colored plastids into chromoplasts. 3. OR transgene increases total carotenoids in potato tubers via carotenoid sequestration structures.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Identified in this review as a bona fide molecular switch for chromoplast formation; acts mainly through sink formation rather than direct transcriptional activation of pathway genes.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor branch related to carotenoid end products and ABA biosynthesis context
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modulate neoxanthin/ABA-related carotenoid composition
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA4 gene product is needed for neoxanthin synthesis. 2. It functions at the final step context of the classical carotenoid pathway.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not described as enzyme class here; treated as common gene due to unclear catalytic assignment in this review.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Mutations cause high-pigment phenotype with elevated carotenoids via altered light signaling/plastid development
Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Fruit-specific suppression in engineering context not specifically assigned to DDB1 here
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Regulatory target for increasing fruit carotenoids, though likely with pleiotropic light-response effects
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DDB1 is a light signaling regulator underlying tomato hp1. 2. Mutation leads to exaggerated photoresponsiveness and elevated carotenoids/flavonoids. 3. Effect is linked to enhanced plastid biogenesis.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts globally through light signaling and plastid development rather than as a direct carotenogenic enzyme.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible broad pleiotropic effects on photomorphogenesis

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Low molecular weight heat shock protein/chaperone
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes chloroplast-to-chromoplast conversion and carotenoid accumulation
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Carotenoid enrichment and oxidative stress-associated plastid protection
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential sink/plastid-development engineering target for carotenoid enhancement
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HSP21 protects photosystem II from oxidative stress. 2. HSP21 promotes chloroplast-to-chromoplast conversion. 3. This plastid transition is associated with increased carotenoid accumulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as plastid-development regulator rather than direct metabolic enzyme.

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with chromoplast development and carotenoid sequestration/accumulation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein characterization and expression association studies
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential targets for increasing sequestration/storage capacity of carotenoids
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Carotenoid-associated proteins such as fibrillin, CHRD and CHRC are linked to chromoplast development. 2. Their expression correlates with carotenoid accumulation. 3. Sequestration structures directly affect carotenoid accumulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Grouped because the review summarizes them collectively as sequestration factors.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Plant; maize; rice; bacterial genes in engineering examples
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Oryza sativa; Solanum lycopersicum; Solanum tuberosum; Brassica napus
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses two molecules of GGDP to form C40 phytoene
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies committed precursor and increases flux toward carotenoid end products
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as rate-limiting/major control step in many plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased PSY expression enhances total carotenoids and β-carotene; PSY3 controls root carotenoid biosynthesis under abiotic stress
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression in canola, tomato, potato; transgenic engineering; metabolite profiling; referenced genetic studies for PSY3
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: PSY3 in maize and rice roots under abiotic stress
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Fruit/tuber/seed when expressed tissue-specifically; roots for PSY3
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: maize PSY used in Golden Rice 2
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Pro-vitamin A nutrition and carotenoid pigmentation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Major target for carotenoid biofortification; can be stacked with crtI or sink-strength genes such as OR
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PSY catalyzes the first committed step in carotenoid biosynthesis. 2. PSY is considered rate-limiting in many plants. 3. Tissue-specific overexpression substantially elevates carotenoids in multiple crops. 4. PSY3 in maize and rice controls root carotenoid biosynthesis under abiotic stress.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Overexpression of tomato PSY1 also affects other isoprenoid metabolism and can induce premature chromoplast-like structures.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can alter other isoprenoid metabolism and plastid type
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Abiotic stress for PSY3; developmental stage in fruits/tubers

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Participates in desaturation of phytoene toward lycopene
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: forms intermediate precursor toward lycopene and downstream carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Necessary for carotenoid biosynthesis; transcript modestly regulated by AtRAP2.2
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced genetic and biochemical studies; promoter regulation context
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastid membrane; found in ~350 kDa complex
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential engineering target within mini-pathways, though not emphasized as primary target in this review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDS is one of the enzymes converting phytoene toward lycopene. 2. PDS occurs in a plastid membrane protein complex. 3. PDS transcript can be modestly affected by AtRAP2.2.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of a hypothesized membrane-associated multi-enzyme complex.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: further desaturated carotene intermediates toward lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Desaturates carotene intermediates in the conversion of phytoene to lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for lycopene formation and downstream carotenoid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Pathway gene for reconstructing carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZDS acts with PDS in desaturation from phytoene toward lycopene. 2. It is part of the core plant desaturation module.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: more trans-configured carotene intermediate(s) toward all-trans-lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts cis-configured carotene intermediates toward all-trans forms during lycopene synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: enables formation of lycopene precursor configuration
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required with CRTISO to convert poly-cis intermediates to all-trans lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Z-ISO is a plant cis-trans isomerase in the lycopene synthesis module. 2. It is required for conversion of poly-cis-configured intermediates toward all-trans lycopene.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes cis-trans isomerization required to produce all-trans lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for production of all-trans lycopene
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Lycopene is an antioxidant beneficial to human health
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRTISO is required for conversion of poly-cis carotene intermediates into all-trans lycopene. 2. It functions with Z-ISO in plant-specific desaturation/isomerization.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Bacterial crtI can replace the function of four plant enzymes including PDS, ZDS, Z-ISO and CRTISO.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Bacterial desaturation/isomerization module replacing multiple plant steps
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Apparently performs desaturation and isomerization reactions equivalent to four plant enzymes to convert phytoene to lycopene
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies lycopene precursor for β-carotene synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Engineering module, not stated as native rate-limiting step
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: In combination with maize PSY enables substantial β-carotene accumulation in rice endosperm; contributes to golden potato engineering
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transgenic metabolic engineering in Golden Rice 2 and potato; carotenoid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Widely useful bacterial module for efficient carotenoid biofortification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Bacterial crtI can replace multiple plant desaturation/isomerization enzymes. 2. Together with maize PSY it enables high β-carotene accumulation in Golden Rice 2. 3. It also supports golden potato engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review explicitly contrasts crtI with four separate plant enzymes.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Lycopene branch selection toward α-carotene/lutein branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes lycopene toward the ε-ring branch leading to α-carotene
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with β-carotene branch and determines branch flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Determines β-carotene/α-carotene ratio; silencing enhances β-carotene accumulation in potato tubers
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Association/genetic evidence; tissue-specific silencing in potato
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: β-carotene as provitamin A; lutein for eye health
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target to redirect flux from α-branch to β-branch for provitamin A enhancement
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCYE controls branch selection at lycopene. 2. It is a key determinant of the β-carotene/α-carotene ratio. 3. Silencing LCYE increases β-carotene in potato tubers.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Harjes et al. cited for key role in branch determination.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Transformation with tomato or bacterial LCYB converts lycopene into β-carotene and produces orange tomato
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Nuclear transformation and plastid transformation; carotenoid profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target for converting lycopene-rich tissues to β-carotene-rich tissues
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCYB converts lycopene to β-carotene. 2. Increased LCYB expression in tomato fruit generates orange tomato with more β-carotene. 3. Bacterial LCYB expression in plastids can achieve a similar effect.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also postulated as part of membrane-associated multienzyme complex with desaturases.

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Carotene β-hydroxylase (non-heme di-iron type and cytochrome P450 type); carotene ε-hydroxylase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates β-rings and ε-rings of carotenes to produce xanthophylls such as lutein and zeaxanthin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: converts provitamin A carotenes into downstream xanthophylls; competes with β-carotene accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Hydroxylation produces lutein and zeaxanthin; silencing β-carotene hydroxylase enhances β-carotene in potato tuber
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway characterization; tissue-specific gene silencing in potato
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastid membrane / ER-associated P450 context not specified in review
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Lutein and zeaxanthin support eye health; zeaxanthin functions in photoprotection
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Silencing β-carotene hydroxylase is useful to raise provitamin A β-carotene
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. β-ring and ε-ring carotene hydroxylases convert α-carotene and β-carotene into lutein and zeaxanthin. 2. Downregulation of β-carotene hydroxylase enhances β-carotene accumulation in potato.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review groups multiple hydroxylases rather than focusing on a single locus.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May lower xanthophylls such as lutein/zeaxanthin

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: converts xanthophyll intermediate and influences xanthophyll cycle/product composition
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Silencing enhances zeaxanthin in potato; mutation in tomato hp3 is associated with increased carotenoid accumulation via larger plastid compartment
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene silencing in potato; mutant analysis in tomato hp3
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Zeaxanthin supports photoprotection and eye health
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Target to increase zeaxanthin or alter xanthophyll composition
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZEP converts zeaxanthin to violaxanthin in the xanthophyll cycle. 2. Silencing ZEP increases zeaxanthin in potato. 3. Tomato hp3, caused by ZEP mutation, shows enhanced carotenoid accumulation linked to increased plastid size.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This review also discusses hp3 as affecting sink capacity/plastid compartment.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May alter ABA-related branch and xanthophyll cycle balance
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High light stress context for xanthophyll cycle

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: De-epoxidizes violaxanthin to zeaxanthin in the xanthophyll cycle
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: directly forms target product in xanthophyll cycle
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces zeaxanthin for the xanthophyll cycle enabling adaptation to high light stress
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for modifying photoprotective xanthophyll cycle capacity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VDE reverses the ZEP reaction to generate zeaxanthin. 2. This cycle helps plants adapt to high light stress.

Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Catalyzes the final step in the classical carotenoid biosynthetic pathway to form neoxanthin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor context for ABA biosynthesis branch
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to modify neoxanthin/ABA precursor pools
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NSY converts violaxanthin to neoxanthin. 2. This is presented as the final step in the classical carotenoid pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A novel gene product ABA4 is also required for neoxanthin synthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Carotenoid catabolism / apocarotenoid and ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively cleaves carotenoids to generate diverse apocarotenoids; NCED cleaves violaxanthin and neoxanthin to form xanthoxin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: apocarotenoids; ABA precursor xanthoxin; carotenoid steady-state levels
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: degrades target-product pool and generates signaling derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: NCED is the first committed step of ABA biosynthesis branch, but not stated here as carotenoid rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CCDs inversely regulate carotenoid accumulation and produce signaling/accessory apocarotenoids; NCED provides xanthoxin for ABA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Referenced gene characterization and expression analyses
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expression induced by environmental stresses; affected by circadian rhythm and development
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Produces developmental signals, antifungal agents, flavor/aroma compounds, and ABA precursor
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Downregulation may increase carotenoid accumulation, but may perturb apocarotenoid/ABA functions
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CCDs catalyze oxidative carotenoid cleavage to apocarotenoids. 2. CCD expression inversely regulates carotenoid accumulation. 3. NCED cleaves violaxanthin and neoxanthin to make xanthoxin for ABA biosynthesis. 4. Carotenoid turnover is induced by stress, circadian rhythm and development.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Cleavage-site and substrate specificity are complex; many CCDs are less substrate-specific.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering CCDs may affect ABA, development, stress responses, flavor/aroma
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Environmental stress; circadian rhythm; developmental cues

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: General plastidic isoprenoid biosynthesis feeding carotenoid pathway
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Adds three IPP molecules to DMAPP to produce GGDP, the common precursor of carotenoids and other plastid isoprenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides central GGDP precursor for carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports synthesis of carotenoids and other isoprenoids
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential precursor-supply target, though branch competition with other isoprenoids must be considered
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGPS produces GGDP, the common precursor for carotenoids and several plastidic isoprenoids. 2. GGPS is part of a soluble plastid protein complex with IPI and PSY for phytoene formation.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: GGDP is also substrate for chlorophyll/tocopherol/phylloquinone and gibberellin branches.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux competition with chlorophyll, tocopherol, phylloquinone and gibberellin biosynthesis

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Interconverts IPP and DMAPP to support GGDP and carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to precursor supply for phytoene formation in plastids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IPI is part of the plastid stromal complex with GGPS and PSY that forms phytoene. 2. It contributes to precursor balance for carotenoid biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyze early steps of the plastid-localized MEP pathway supplying IPP and DMAPP for carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provide upstream precursor supply for carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports carotenoid and other plastid isoprenoid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Upstream engineering targets, but broad flux effects expected
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXS and DXR are early MEP-pathway enzymes that provide IPP/DMAPP for carotenoid biosynthesis. 2. DXP is also a branch point to thiamine and pyridoxal biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Flux from DXP also branches to thiamine and pyridoxal.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Affects multiple isoprenoid and vitamin pathways

Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Geranylgeranyl diphosphate reductase; ent-copalyl diphosphate synthase; ent-kaurene synthase
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: phytol (GGR); ent-copalyl diphosphate and ent-kaurene pathway intermediates (CPS/KS)
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Consume GGDP for chlorophyll/tocopherol/phylloquinone side chain formation or gibberellin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Divert GGDP away from carotenoid biosynthesis into other plastid isoprenoid and gibberellin pathways
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Highlights need to consider branch competition when engineering carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GGR, CPS and KS represent major side branches competing for GGDP. 2. These branches influence precursor availability for carotenoid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review emphasizes branch competition in metabolic engineering context.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may affect chlorophyll, tocopherol, phylloquinone or gibberellin biosynthesis and plant growth

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Binds a main regulatory region of the PSY promoter and modestly increases transcript levels of PSY and PDS
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Conditionally positive regulator but not described as master regulator
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Promoter binding study; transcript analysis; carotenoid measurement
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulatory target to tune carotenoid pathway gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtRAP2.2 binds a main regulatory region of the PSY promoter. 2. It modestly regulates PSY and PDS transcript levels. 3. It modestly affects carotenoid content in Arabidopsis.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One of only a few transcription factors cited as directly regulating carotenoid metabolism.

Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Global control of carotenoid accumulation through light signaling and plastid development
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Negative regulation; suppression or mutation increases pigments
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly enhances carotenoid accumulation when DET1 function is reduced
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss or fruit-specific suppression increases carotenoids and flavonoids in tomato fruit
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major negative regulator in high-pigment phenotype and engineering target
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis (hp2); fruit-specific suppression transgenics; metabolite profiling
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful engineering target for increasing carotenoids, though broad light-signaling pleiotropy should be considered
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DET1 is a light signaling regulator underlying tomato hp2. 2. Reduced DET1 function causes exaggerated photoresponsiveness and elevated carotenoids/flavonoids. 3. Fruit-specific suppression enhances carotenoid accumulation in tomato fruits.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Likely acts through plastid development and global light signaling rather than direct promoter binding of carotenogenic genes.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad pleiotropic effects from altered light signaling

Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirect positive effect on pigment accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression increases carotenoid and flavonoid levels in tomato fruits
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Useful positive regulator in engineering context
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering option for enhancing carotenoids through photoreceptor signaling
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRY2 is a blue-light photoreceptor. 2. Overexpression of CRY2 increases carotenoids and flavonoids in tomato fruits.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as an example of using light signaling genes to engineer carotenoids.
